events.note: Writing Intent Check (revision 1) for the netpath review fixes task

events.change_summary: Intent Check: seven review fixes — traceroute fallback honoured, country-ranking memory blowup removed, Globalping verdicts and auth errors, git hygiene, Atlas docs/code reconciliation

events.summary: Intent Check (revision 1) pushed: seven review fixes covering the mtr/traceroute fallback gate, hardcoded traceroute path, country-ranking memory blowup, missing Globalping verdicts, swallowed auth errors, git-tracked generated files, and Atlas docs/code reconciliation.

events.note: Writing full scope as revision 2 after approved intent check

events.change_summary: Full scope (revision 2): 14 requirements and 12 acceptance criteria covering the seven review findings, organized into three phases — behaviour fixes, Atlas dead-code removal, and bulk git untracking. Grounded in codebase exploration: atlas.py confirmed production-dead (test-only importer), _trace() identified as needing an mtr-absent branch beyond the existing permission-error fallback.

events.rationale: Seven review-surfaced bug fixes in a CLI tool: no UI exists, so design is skipped. Each fix is well-contained to 1-2 files following existing patterns (dependency gate, binary resolution, arithmetic replacement, verdict recomputation, exception surfacing, file deletion, git index cleanup) — no new system boundaries, so architecture is skipped; detailed per-phase Files/Verification are in the scope instead. Review is included because the diagnose()-on-remote-only-rows change and the prober-gate rework carry behavioural risk.

alternatives_considered: Keep atlas.py but strip it to target-discovery helpers and rename it (rejected: country.py already owns that logic independently, so the module would be empty)
Leave atlas.py and soften the README claim instead (rejected: keeps dead key-based scheduling code that contradicts the removal decision already made)
events.decision: Delete src/netpath/atlas.py and tests/test_atlas.py outright rather than keeping atlas.py as a renamed/narrower module
events.rationale: Codebase exploration shows atlas.py is imported only by tests/test_atlas.py — no production module uses it. The surviving keyless trace-target discovery (_get_atlas_probe_ip in country.py) calls the public Atlas probes API directly and does not import atlas.py, so deletion changes no production behaviour and makes the README's "fully removed" claim true. Docstrings in country.py and a README sentence will document the narrower surviving lookup.

alternatives_considered: Return None vs [] from fetch_probes to signal auth failure (rejected: ambiguous at the call site and easy to conflate with empty coverage)
Fold the git cleanup into Phase 1 (rejected: the mechanical bulk diff would overwhelm review of the behavioural changes)
events.decision: Surface Globalping auth failure from fetch_probes() via a distinct exception (GlobalpingAuthError) rather than a sentinel return value; isolate the bulk git rm --cached into its own final phase
events.rationale: An exception keeps the existing "return [] on failure" contract intact for non-auth errors and mirrors the CLI's existing scheduling-time 401 handling at cli.py:849-855, giving the call site one obvious catch point. The git untracking produces a diff of thousands of index-only deletions; combining it with the code fixes would bury the substantive review, so it lands as a dedicated Phase 3 commit with no source edits.

events.summary: Full scope pushed as revision 2: 14 requirements, 12 acceptance criteria, 3 phases (behaviour fixes; Atlas dead-code removal; bulk git untracking). Workflow classified as bug-fix / standard, skipping design and architecture. Two decisions logged: delete atlas.py outright (confirmed production-dead — only tests/test_atlas.py imports it), and raise a distinct GlobalpingAuthError from fetch_probes(). Exploration also found the fix for the mtr gate needs a second change beyond _check_deps(): _trace() only falls back on MtrPermissionError, so it needs an mtr-absent branch too — captured as R2.

events.rationale: Routine sync drift: HEAD fast-forwarded (stale HEAD is a strict ancestor of current) with a byte-identical dirty-status digest; swept forward automatically without waking an agent.

events.note: Starting Phase 1: behaviour fixes for probing, ranking, and remote verdicts

events.description: Starting phase 1: Behaviour fixes for probing, ranking, and remote verdicts
Implementing core changes: prober gate, traceroute resolution, host arithmetic, remote verdicts, auth error

events.details: pytest: 162 passed (153 baseline + 9 new: first-host arithmetic incl. /8+/31+/32+malformed, prober gate pass/exit, _trace mtr-absent fallback, remote-only diagnose healthy/loss, fetch_probes 401/403/404). ruff check src tests: clean. Live smoke: with mtr.available patched False, _check_deps passed and _trace completed a real 9-hop traceroute fallback.

alternatives_considered: Module-level global with manual memoization (more code, same effect)
Caching mtr.available() too (rejected: breaks the established monkeypatch pattern in tests for negligible gain)
events.decision: Implementation: traceroute resolution lives in mtr.traceroute_path() with functools.lru_cache(maxsize=1), exposed via mtr.traceroute_available() for the CLI gate
events.rationale: R4 + technical note ask for one-time resolution rather than re-running shutil.which per hop-trace in a country sweep. lru_cache gives per-process caching with a test-friendly cache_clear(); the /usr/sbin/traceroute path survives only as an existence-and-executability-checked constant inside the resolver, never in command construction. mtr.available() and paris.detect() stay uncached: they are single which() calls and existing tests monkeypatch them directly.

alternatives_considered: Guard clauses inside diagnose() for remote-only rows (rejected: existing defensive reads already handle the shape)
Raising MtrPermissionError from the ECMP branch to reuse its fallback handler (rejected: misuses the exception semantics)
events.decision: Implementation: remote-only verdicts achieved by dropping the verdict-key gate on the post-merge recompute; diagnosis.py needed no changes. ECMP branch gated with 'and mtr.available()' so mtr-absent runs fall into the single-pass fallback branch. Auth-failure wording branches on token presence.
events.rationale: diagnose() already reads every key defensively via .get(): a row with no hubs/path_complete/probe_count produces no spurious signals (path_complete absent yields None, not False, so no incomplete_path alarm) — verified by the two new remote-only tests. For ECMP, when mtr is absent the elif falls through to the existing _trace branch which routes to the fallback prober; ECMP comparison is simply skipped, matching the existing MtrPermissionError behaviour. For auth failures with no token supplied (anonymous 401, should not happen), the message says 'rejected the probe inventory request as unauthorized' instead of blaming a nonexistent token; per-row probe_errors use 'invalid Globalping token' only when a token was supplied.

events.change_summary: Phase 1 complete: Behaviour fixes for probing, ranking, and remote verdicts

events.summary: Fixed the five runtime defects: dependency gate now exits only when no path prober exists at all (with an error naming mtr and traceroute install options); _trace() and the ECMP branch route to the fallback prober when mtr is absent; traceroute binary resolved via shutil.which with /usr/sbin/traceroute as an existence-checked last resort; country ranking derives sample IPs arithmetically instead of materializing list(net.hosts()); remote-only Globalping rows now receive diagnose() verdicts that render in the summary and drive the exit code; fetch_probes() raises GlobalpingAuthError on 401/403 so an invalid token is reported as an auth failure instead of missing coverage. 162 tests pass (9 new), ruff clean, live fallback smoke-tested.

items.description: Simulate a machine without mtr: run `env PATH="/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin" .venv/bin/netpath asn AS13335 --no-throughput -n 1` (this PATH hides Homebrew's mtr but keeps `/usr/sbin/traceroute`). Expect the run to complete with the notice `(mtr unavailable — using traceroute + Cymru ASN lookup)` instead of exiting 1.

Normal path unchanged: run `netpath country US --top 3 --no-throughput` with mtr installed and confirm it completes with the usual mtr-based traces, the ranking step finishes promptly (US allocations include very wide prefixes), and the summary renders.

Invalid token reported as auth failure: run `netpath country US --top 3 --no-throughput --gp-token invalid-token-123` and confirm it prints `Globalping rejected the token from --gp-token / NETPATH_GLOBALPING_TOKEN — skipping remote measurements` and does NOT print `No Globalping probes found in any target ASN`.

Note: netpath reads the token from `NETPATH_GLOBALPING_TOKEN`, not `GLOBALPING_TOKEN`. If you exported `GLOBALPING_TOKEN` in your shell, re-export it as `export NETPATH_GLOBALPING_TOKEN="$GLOBALPING_TOKEN"` before token-based runs, or pass `--gp-token` explicitly.

Remote-only verdict reaches the exit code: with the token set, run `netpath country US --top 10 --no-throughput` on a country where some ISP has no iperf3/trace target but Globalping coverage; confirm the remote-only summary row shows `verdict ... (near-target)` in its Globalping subrow, then check `echo $?` reflects the worst verdict (0 ok / 1 warning / 2 critical).

No-prober error message: run `env PATH="/usr/bin:/bin" .venv/bin/python -c "from netpath import cli; cli._check_deps(True)"` — with mtr, Paris probers, and traceroute all hidden it should print the error naming both `mtr` and `traceroute` install options and raise exit 1 (on macOS `/usr/sbin/traceroute` still resolves via the existence check, so this may pass the gate instead — that is expected).

events.summary: Retired the dead Atlas measurement backend: deleted src/netpath/atlas.py (key-based scheduling, credits, polling — production-dead, imported only by its own test file) and tests/test_atlas.py. Documented the surviving keyless Atlas probe lookup in country.py docstrings (_get_atlas_probe_ip, get_test_target_for_asn) as a public no-key trace-target discovery only. README now names RIPE Atlas as the removed backend and clarifies the one surviving keyless touchpoint. 155 tests pass, ruff clean, no netpath.atlas references remain.

items.description: Confirm the dead module is gone: `ls src/netpath/atlas.py tests/test_atlas.py` reports both as No such file, and `grep -rn "netpath.atlas\|from .atlas" src tests` returns nothing.

Production behaviour unchanged: run `netpath country US --top 3 --no-throughput` and confirm trace-target discovery still works — rows resolved through the surviving lookup show `Atlas probe trace target` as the target origin, exactly as before.

Read the `Upgrading from earlier versions` section of `README.md` and confirm it now names RIPE Atlas as the removed backend and states that a public keyless probe lookup survives solely for discovering live trace targets.

Read the docstrings of `_get_atlas_probe_ip` and `get_test_target_for_asn` in `src/netpath/country.py` and confirm they describe the lookup as public, keyless, and used only as a trace target with no measurements scheduled.
Run `pytest` (expect 155 passed — 7 fewer than Phase 1's 162, matching the deleted Atlas test file) and `ruff check src tests` (expect clean).

workflow.rationale: Seven review-surfaced bug fixes in a CLI tool: no UI exists, so design is skipped. Each fix is well-contained to 1-2 files following existing patterns (dependency gate, binary resolution, arithmetic replacement, verdict recomputation, exception surfacing, file deletion, git index cleanup) — no new system boundaries, so architecture is skipped; detailed per-phase Files/Verification are in the scope instead. Review is included because the diagnose()-on-remote-only-rows change and the prober-gate rework carry behavioural risk.

stageContent.implementation:
## Phase 1: Behaviour fixes for probing, ranking, and remote verdicts

### What was built

**Prober fallback (R1-R5)**
- `mtr.py`: new `traceroute_path()` resolver (PATH lookup via `shutil.which`, then an existence-and-executability check on `/usr/sbin/traceroute` for macOS shells; cached with `functools.lru_cache`) and `traceroute_available()`. `_run_traceroute_cmd()` now uses the resolved binary and raises a clear "traceroute binary not found" error when nothing resolves — no hardcoded path in command construction.
- `cli.py` `_check_deps()`: exits only when no prober exists at all (mtr absent AND `paris.detect()` None AND traceroute unresolvable); the error names both install options.
- `cli.py` `_trace()`: routes directly to `_fallback_trace()` when `mtr.available()` is false, alongside the existing `MtrPermissionError` path. The ECMP branch in `_measure()` is gated with `and mtr.available()` so multi-pass runs without mtr fall into the single-pass fallback branch (ECMP comparison skipped, same as the existing permission-error behaviour). The existing "(mtr unavailable — using traceroute/Paris + Cymru ASN lookup)" notice covers R3 unchanged.

**Country ranking memory (R6)**
- `country.py` `get_top_asns()`: sample IP derived arithmetically — `net.network_address + 1` for /30 and wider, the network address itself for /31 and /32. `list(net.hosts())` is gone; `ip_to_size` still uses `net.num_addresses`. Output is byte-identical to the old `hosts[0]` for all prefix lengths.

**Remote-only verdicts (R7, R8)**
- `cli.py`: the post-merge verdict recompute no longer requires an existing verdict key — any row whose Globalping loss or jitter merged gets `diagnose()` run, so remote-only rows gain verdicts, render `verdict X (near-target)` in the summary subrow (display support already existed), and flow into the exit-code collection unchanged.
- `diagnosis.py` needed no changes: all reads are defensive `.get()` calls, and a missing `path_complete` key yields None (not False), so no spurious incomplete-path signal. Verified by tests.

**Globalping auth (R9, R10)**
- `globalping.py`: new `GlobalpingAuthError(RuntimeError)`; `fetch_probes()` raises it on HTTP 401/403 and keeps the `return []` contract for every other failure.
- `cli.py` country: the inventory fetch is wrapped in try/except; auth failure prints the token-rejected message (worded per token presence — anonymous 401s do not blame a nonexistent token), suppresses the misleading "No Globalping probes found" line, and records "invalid Globalping token" / "Globalping authentication failed" on all rows instead of "no Globalping coverage". The `coverage` command also catches the error and exits 1 instead of tracebacking.

### Deviations from plan
None. README needed no Phase 1 touch-up — the line 23 claim ("falls back to traceroute if unavailable") is now accurate as written.

### Files changed
- src/netpath/mtr.py
- src/netpath/cli.py
- src/netpath/country.py
- src/netpath/globalping.py
- tests/test_country.py (+5 tests)
- tests/test_diagnosis.py (+2 tests)
- tests/test_globalping.py (+3 tests)

### Verification
- pytest: 162 passed (153 baseline + 9 new), ruff clean
- Live smoke: with `mtr.available` patched False, `_check_deps` passed and `_trace` completed a real 9-hop traceroute fallback via `/usr/sbin/traceroute`
- Greps: no `list(net.hosts())` in country.py; `/usr/sbin/traceroute` appears only as the existence-checked `_TRACEROUTE_SBIN` constant

## Phase 2: Retire the dead Atlas measurement code and reconcile the docs

### What was built

**Atlas dead-code removal (R12)**
- Deleted `src/netpath/atlas.py` (316 lines: key-based scheduling, credit checks, result polling/parsing) and `tests/test_atlas.py` (its only importer — verified again this phase: no reference in `src/netpath/__init__.py`, `pyproject.toml`, or any production module). Removed via `git rm` so the deletions are staged for the approval commit.

**Surviving keyless lookup documented (R13)**
- `country.py` `_get_atlas_probe_ip()` docstring now states it is a public, keyless lookup against the RIPE Atlas probes API — no key, credits, or account — used only as a trace target, and is the sole remnant of the removed Atlas measurement backend.
- `country.py` `get_test_target_for_asn()` docstring similarly clarifies the Atlas-preferred target comes from a keyless public query and that no Atlas measurements are scheduled.
- The "Atlas probe trace target" display strings in cli.py (lines 779, 818, 820) are unchanged — they accurately describe this surviving lookup.

**README reconciliation (R14)**
- The "Upgrading from earlier versions" paragraph now names RIPE Atlas as the removed backend (making "fully removed" true as of this phase) and adds a sentence that one narrow touchpoint survives: a public, keyless lookup of the RIPE Atlas probes API in country mode, solely to discover a live trace-target address — no key, credits, or measurements involved.
- The traceroute-fallback claim at README line 23 was verified accurate against the Phase 1 gate behaviour; no change needed.

### Deviations from plan
None.

### Files changed
- src/netpath/atlas.py (deleted)
- tests/test_atlas.py (deleted)
- src/netpath/country.py (docstrings only)
- README.md

### Verification
- pytest: 155 passed (162 baseline minus the 7 deleted Atlas tests), ruff clean
- `grep -r "netpath.atlas\|from .atlas" src tests` returns nothing; both files gone from the working tree
- README section on the removed backend mentions the surviving keyless probe-address lookup
